paragraphs.text: Child Street 4 1217 Copenhagen K
www.danskerhverv.dk info@danskerhverv.dk +45 3374 6000
DANIS PROFESS IONS
21 October 2025

section_path: Orientation

paragraphs.text: The Danish Chamber of Commerce is grateful for the opportunity to respond to the open consultation of the European Commission on the forthcoming Digital Fairness Act (DFA). As a representative of Danish business and a key part of the European business sector, the Danish Business Authority wishes to contribute constructively to the development of an effective and balanced set of rules for the digitale economy.

section_path: Danish Business Authority’s response to the consultation on the Commission’s call on the future Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: Dansk Erhverv shares the Commission’s ambition to strengthen consumer protection and build confidence in digital markets. At the same time, it is our clear assessment that the current EU legal framework already provides a comprehensive and flexible toolbox to deal with the pro rata positions the DFA seeks to address. The challenge lies not in a regulatory vacuum, but in a lack of uniform enforcement and interpretation across Member States.

section_path: Danish Business Authority’s response to the consultation on the Commission’s call on the future Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: A new regulation risks increasing complexity and creating further fragmentation rather than addressing the real challenges. Dansk Erhverv therefore recommends that the Commission should instead give priority to strengthened enforcement, closer coordination and clearer guidance on the application of existence rules.

section_path: Danish Business Authority’s response to the consultation on the Commission’s call on the future Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: In recent years, the EU has adopted a number of key digital frameworks, including the Digital Services Act (DSA), the Digital Markets Act (DMA), the AI Act, the Data Act and the Modernisation Directive. These core-sets complement existing consumer and marketing rules and together form a solid legal framework for a safe and fair digital single market.

section_path: Danish Business Authority’s response to the consultation on the Commission’s call on the future Digital Fairness Act
Proportionality and the need for coherence

paragraphs.text: However, several of these rules are still being implemented and their impact cannot yet be fully achieved. The Commission’s own analyses – including Fitness Check 2024 – suggest that the problem is uneven enforcement, overlapping requirements and a lack of clarity in the border between the many sets of rules.

section_path: Danish Business Authority’s response to the consultation on the Commission’s call on the future Digital Fairness Act
Proportionality and the need for coherence

paragraphs.text: It is therefore essential that the Commission maintains the principle of proportionality so that new legislation is only put into effect where there is a proven need. A more coherent and toosightful regulatory system will benefit consumers, authorities and businesses alike. Dansk Erhverv therefore recommends that the Commission focus on enforcement, coordination and guidance, rather than introducing a new legislative layer.

section_path: Danish Business Authority’s response to the consultation on the Commission’s call on the future Digital Fairness Act
Proportionality and the need for coherence

evidence_quotes.quote: The concept of deceptive patterns is increasingly used to denote deceptive or minor designs in digital user interfaces.

paragraphs.text: The concept of deceptive patterns is increasingly used to denote deceptive or minor designs in digital user interfaces. While recognising the need to shield consumers from undue influence, Dansk Erhverv considers that most of these practices are already regulated through existing EU law instruments.

section_path: Danish Business Authority’s response to the consultation on the Commission’s call on the future Digital Fairness Act
Deceptive patterns and design practices

evidence_quotes.quote: consent for binding personalisation and targeting.

paragraphs.text: The Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (UCPD) prohibits unfair and misleading commercial practices, the Digital Services Act sets out specific transparency requirements for digital user resorts, and the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) regulates data processing and consent for binding personalisation and targeting.

section_path: Danish Business Authority’s response to the consultation on the Commission’s call on the future Digital Fairness Act
Deceptive patterns and design practices

paragraphs.text: MJO

section_path: Danish Business Authority’s response to the consultation on the Commission’s call on the future Digital Fairness Act
Deceptive patterns and design practices

evidence_quotes.quote: the term ‘deceptive patterns’ is used without a clear definition.
Personalised marketing is a key and legitimate element of the digital economy.

paragraphs.text: The problem is not the lack of legislation, but the term ‘deceptive patterns’ is used without a clear definition. This creates a risk of divergent interpretation and unpredictable requirements. Dansk Erhverv recommends that the Commission should base itself on the definition in Article 67 DSA, which limits the concept to practices that materially impair the consumer’s ability to make informed choices. Personalised marketing and data use Personalised marketing is a key and legitimate element of the digital economy. It enables businesses to communicate in a targeted manner with consumers, which both increase the habit of advertising and enhances competitiveness – especially for small and medium-sized enterprises that rely on cost-effective marketing tools.

section_path: Danish Business Authority’s response to the consultation on the Commission’s call on the future Digital Fairness Act
Deceptive patterns and design practices

evidence_quotes.quote: including consent and the right to object to profiling.
The DMA contains additional restrictions on data exchange between large digital platforms.

paragraphs.text: The current rules already provide strong protection for consumers’ rights. The GDPRegulates the collection and processing of personal data, including consent and the right to object to profiling. The DSA limits the use of sensitive data and lays down specific protection requirements for minors. The DMA contains additional restrictions on data exchange between large digital platforms.

section_path: Danish Business Authority’s response to the consultation on the Commission’s call on the future Digital Fairness Act
Deceptive patterns and design practices

evidence_quotes.quote: New restrictions or bans on personalised marketing will create uncertainty and weaken companies’ ability to communicate effectively with their customers.

paragraphs.text: Dansk Erhverv considers that these instruments together constitute adequate protection. New restrictions or bans on personalised marketing will create uncertainty and weaken companies’ ability to communicate effectively with their customers. Instead, the KomMission should strengthen guidance on how existing rules interact and ensure that consumers are provided with clear information on how their data is used.

section_path: Danish Business Authority’s response to the consultation on the Commission’s call on the future Digital Fairness Act
Deceptive patterns and design practices

paragraphs.text: Dansk Erhverv wishes to warn against proposals that regulate companies’ design choices in detail. User interfaces and layouts are important parameters of competition that support innovation and quality development. Gold-plating of visual or functional elements – such as requiring specific buttons or layouts – will limit companies’ freedom to adapt to different equal markets and products.

section_path: Danish Business Authority’s response to the consultation on the Commission’s call on the future Digital Fairness Act
Freedom of design and user experience

paragraphs.text: Regulation should therefore focus on openness and transparency, not on the definition of kon-cretedesign solutions. The Commission’s aim should be to ensure that users can make informed choices, while preserving flexibility for companies to develop intuitive, userequal and competitive solutions.

section_path: Danish Business Authority’s response to the consultation on the Commission’s call on the future Digital Fairness Act
Freedom of design and user experience

evidence_quotes.quote: Pricing and price information are at the heart of consumer protection, but also an area where overlapping regulation and diverging national practices create challenges.
The rules of the Price Indication Directive and the UCPD already provide a solid framework for communicating prices and discounts.

paragraphs.text: Pricing and price information are at the heart of consumer protection, but also an area where overlapping regulation and diverging national practices create challenges. The rules of the Price Indication Directive and the UCPD already provide a solid framework for communicating prices and discounts.

section_path: Danish Business Authority’s response to the consultation on the Commission’s call on the future Digital Fairness Act
Pricing and transparency

paragraphs.text: Dansk Erhverv recommends that the Commission focus on creating uniformity of interpretation across Member States rather than extending regulation. At the same time, the rules should remain flexible enough to accommodate new forms of pricing, such as dynamic prices and personalised offers, as long as the information is clear and not misleading.

section_path: Danish Business Authority’s response to the consultation on the Commission’s call on the future Digital Fairness Act
Pricing and transparency

evidence_quotes.quote: Subscription-based business models are an important part of the digital economy and offer flexibility and predictability to consumers.

paragraphs.text: Subscription-based business models are an important part of the digital economy and offer flexibility and predictability to consumers. Danske Erhverv supports the objective of ensuring that progress is made, but considers it essential that the regulation takes account of the wide variation in themedels.

section_path: Danish Business Authority’s response to the consultation on the Commission’s call on the future Digital Fairness Act
Subscription models and consumer information

evidence_quotes.quote: Requiring fixed notification patterns or mandatory opt-in mechanisms can be unnecessarily burdensome and create confusion for consumers.

paragraphs.text: Requiring fixed notification patterns or mandatory opt-in mechanisms can be unnecessarily burdensome and create confusion for consumers. Consumers receiving regular living rances are already aware of their contract. Similarly, companies with flexible forms of abode should be able to provide information on how the price is calculated instead of disclosing a fixed annual price range.

section_path: Danish Business Authority’s response to the consultation on the Commission’s call on the future Digital Fairness Act
Subscription models and consumer information

paragraphs.text: Page 2/4

section_path: Danish Business Authority’s response to the consultation on the Commission’s call on the future Digital Fairness Act
Subscription models and consumer information

evidence_quotes.quote: clear information and easy access to termination.

paragraphs.text: The existing rules of the Consumer Rights Directive already require clear information and easy access to termination. The Commission should therefore concentrate on clarifying how these rules are to be understood and applied in practice.

section_path: Danish Business Authority’s response to the consultation on the Commission’s call on the future Digital Fairness Act
Subscription models and consumer information

paragraphs.text: A key challenge in EU consumer law is different enforcement and interpretation between Member States. Dansk Erhverv therefore calls on the Commission to strengthen cooperation through the Consumer Protection Cooperations network and to prioritise the development of common interpretation and practical guidance.

section_path: Danish Business Authority’s response to the consultation on the Commission’s call on the future Digital Fairness Act
Enforcement and cooperation

paragraphs.text: The development of casestudy-based examples of how existing rules are applied in concrete situations will provide a more uniform starting point for both businesses and authorities. Such an approach would bring real improvement to consumers and enhance legal certainty without further complicating the legal landscape.

section_path: Danish Business Authority’s response to the consultation on the Commission’s call on the future Digital Fairness Act
Enforcement and cooperation

paragraphs.text: Dansk Erhverv recommends that the European Commission refrain from proposing a new Digital Fairness Act. The EU already has a comprehensive and well-developed regulatory framework that ensures a high level of consumer protection, while allowing businesses to unlock new products and services.

section_path: Danish Business Authority’s response to the consultation on the Commission’s call on the future Digital Fairness Act
Final remarks

paragraphs.text: The main challenge is not the absence of rules but the lack of uniform application and effective enforcement. A new legislative layer would not solve this problem, but rather increase Complexity. Instead, the Commission should focus on strengthening cooperation between national authorities, developing clearer guidance, promoting legal coherence between existing regulatory frameworks and supporting stronger dialogue with business.

section_path: Danish Business Authority’s response to the consultation on the Commission’s call on the future Digital Fairness Act
Final remarks

paragraphs.text: A clear, coherent and effectively enforced framework will provide more legal certainty, strengthen consumer confidence and create better conditions for innovation and growth in the European poem of speech economy.

section_path: Danish Business Authority’s response to the consultation on the Commission’s call on the future Digital Fairness Act
Final remarks

paragraphs.text: Happy regards

section_path: Danish Business Authority’s response to the consultation on the Commission’s call on the future Digital Fairness Act
Final remarks
